feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Abdul-Samad Ahmed — Kinesiology student and researcher at the University of Calgary.

entry.title: Welcome to My Blog
entry.content:
Hello World!

Welcome to my new blog section. This is the very first post on the site! I’ll be using this space to share my thoughts, updates, and more. Stay tuned for future posts!
entry.summary: My first ever blog post on the new site!

entry.title: The Dry Run
entry.content:
It was an absolutely freezing morning today, the kind where you just want to stay under the covers and ignore your alarms. But once I got into the Herzog Lab for our group presentation, something just clicked. I was scheduled to give a standard progress update, but I made a last-minute executive decision: I hijacked my own time slot to basically do a full dry run of my poster presentation instead.

It was a bit of a gamble, but I am so glad I did it because I was absolutely on fire today.

Thinking back to that rough update presentation I had a few months ago, this felt like night and day. The biggest difference was my pacing. I forced myself to be slower and significantly more composed. I did not just rush through the slides to get it over with; I actually let the data breathe and gave myself the space to explain the mechanics properly. By slowing down, I found that the natural enthusiasm I actually have for this project finally started bleeding through into my delivery.

The Q&A section was the real test, though. In the past, getting grilled on the methodology would spike my heart rate, but today I felt anchored. I was able to answer the questions transparently—if I didn’t have a perfect answer, I didn’t try to mask it, but I leaned into what the data was telling us with a much higher degree of confidence. Not an arrogant “I know everything” kind of confidence, but a grounded trust in the work I have put in over the last year.

Walking out of the lab today, the cold didn’t really bother me. It feels incredibly good to see tangible progress, not just in the data collection, but in how I am communicating it!
entry.summary: Turning a standard Herzog Lab update into a full poster presentation dry run.

entry.title: New Year, New Life
entry.content:
2025 was genuinely one of the most intense years of my life. Between the symposiums, the fiber isolation practice sessions, the Kananaskis trip, and that December presentation that humbled me more than I expected, it was a year that pushed me in ways I did not fully anticipate when it started.

But I am glad it did.

I came into my honors thesis thinking I had a solid handle on what the year would look like. And in a lot of ways, I did. The research itself has been exactly as fascinating as I hoped. The myofilament mechanics, the data pipelines, the long hours in the lab — all of it has been deeply rewarding. What I did not fully account for was how much the non-science parts of research would challenge me. The communication. The presentations. The ability to make a room feel your enthusiasm instead of just telling them about it.

That December presentation was a wake-up call I needed. I have been carrying that feedback with me into the new year and I am determined to do something productive with it.

So here is what 2026 looks like from where I am standing: the data collection phase of my thesis is in full swing, and the finish line is closer than it has ever been. I have a poster presentation coming up that I plan to absolutely nail. And I am going into it with a completely different mindset than I had a few months ago — slower, more grounded, and ready to actually let the passion I have for this work show through.

New year, new life. Let’s finish this strong. 🔬🎯
entry.summary: Reflecting on everything 2025 threw at me and setting my sights on what 2026 needs to be.

entry.title: The Update Presentation
entry.content:
So I had my thesis update presentation today. The idea was to walk everyone through all the prep work I have done so far, the experimental protocol, the Excel pipeline I built, and where things stand with data collection. I spent a lot of time getting the slides together and making sure the content was solid. I genuinely felt prepared going into it.

It did not go the way I wanted.

Looking back, I know I spoke way too fast. I tend to do that when I am nervous and today was no exception. I just kept pushing through slide after slide without giving anything room to breathe. I also know I was not animated enough. I was so focused on getting through the material that I probably came across as flat. Stiff. Like I was just reading bullet points and trying to get it over with.

The comment that stung the most was hearing that it felt like I was uninterested and wanted to leave. That one genuinely hurt. Because the reality could not be further from the truth. I love this project. I think about it constantly. The mechanics of the papillary muscle, the data we are generating, all of it fascinates me. But somehow when I get up in front of people, all of that excitement just does not translate. It gets buried under nerves and I end up sounding monotone.

It is frustrating because I know the enthusiasm is there. I just struggle to let it show when I am presenting. It is something I have always dealt with and clearly still need to work on.

I would be lying if I said this did not make me question my competence a little. When you put real effort into something and the feedback suggests otherwise, it is hard not to internalize that. But I am choosing to take this in stride. One rough presentation does not define the quality of my work or how much I care about it. If anything, it is just another thing to grow from. Slow down, be more expressive, let the passion come through. I will get there. 💪
entry.summary: Reflecting on a thesis update presentation that did not go the way I hoped.

entry.title: Beginning My Thesis Experiments
entry.content:
After all the months of practicing fiber isolation and getting comfortable at the dissecting scope, I am beyond excited to say that the real thesis experiments have officially begun! 🎉

Here is the best part: I do not have to mount the fibers myself. That was honestly one of the things I was most nervous about going in. Mounting a single muscle fiber onto the testing apparatus without damaging it is incredibly delicate work, and after watching Venus do it so effortlessly while I struggled through practice sessions, I was not exactly brimming with confidence. But my role in the experiment flow means I get to skip that step entirely and focus on the actual data collection and analysis side of things. I am not going to lie, that is a huge relief.

Now for the part that has been keeping my brain busy: the math. The mechanical testing generates raw force and length data, but turning that into meaningful stress-strain relationships and deriving the actual material properties of the myofilaments involves some pretty intense calculations. We are talking about converting raw force outputs into stress by accounting for cross-sectional area, calculating sarcomere length changes, and then fitting the data to exponential and polynomial models to extract passive and active force-length characteristics. There are a lot of moving pieces.

Rather than crunching all of that by hand or writing scripts from scratch, I built out a comprehensive Excel sheet with all the formulae baked in. You plug in your raw data and it handles the unit conversions, the cross-sectional area normalization, the stress-strain calculations, and even generates the key plots automatically. It took a solid chunk of time to set up and validate, but now that it is running, it saves an enormous amount of time per experiment. One sheet, all the math, no room for manual calculation errors. It is honestly one of the things I am most proud of so far in this project.

The first few experimental runs have gone smoothly and I am starting to see real data come together. There is nothing quite like watching months of preparation finally translate into actual results on a screen. This is what I have been working toward all year and it feels amazing to finally be here. Time to generate some serious data. 🔬📊
entry.summary: The real experiments are finally starting and I am thrilled to get my hands on actual data.

entry.title: Back from Kananaskis
entry.content:
I am finally back home and settled after the conference in Kananaskis. The whole experience was honestly so inspiring. Getting away from the lab for a few days to see what everyone else is working on really recharged my batteries.

The symposium was a blast from start to finish. I really enjoyed the different presentations, and I actually found myself developing a budding interest in advanced footwear technology. It is a cool intersection of Kinesiology and engineering that I hadn’t given much thought to before. Aside from the academics, the food was great, and the midnight adventures with the group were definitely a highlight. We ended up watching baseball at one point, and even though I still have absolutely no understanding of how that sport works, the vibes were top tier.

One of the best parts was waking up early for a sunrise hike. Seeing the sun come up over the mountains was incredible and definitely made the early alarm worth it. Got some incredicly cool shots of the car too ^_^

The drive back was just as good as the drive up. The mountain scenery never gets old, and I even spotted a BMW M8 Competition on the road, which was a nice bonus for the drive. I also managed to find some of the darkest coffee I’ve ever tasted to keep me fueled for the trip home. It was the perfect way to wrap up a long weekend before getting back into the thick of my honors work.
entry.summary: A reflection on the Alberta Biomedical Engineering symposium in Kananaskis and the drive home.

entry.title: Gone to Kannanaskis
entry.content:
The bags are officially packed and the conference fits are laid out. I am completely prepped and ready to head out for the Alberta Biomedical Engineering symposium. The poster is printed and safely stored in its tube, so there is no turning back now. The practice runs definitely paid off and I am feeling pretty confident about the presentation.

I am also genuinely excited for the trip up there. I volunteered to be the driver for a few friends from the lab and the whip is gassed up and ready to hit the mountains. Being the designated driver means I have absolute veto power over the road trip playlist, which is a massive win. Plus, cruising through Kananaskis is the perfect way to clear my head and relax a bit before jumping into full academic mode. Really hoping to get some nice shots of the mountains!

Fingers crossed everyone packed light so we can actually fit all our luggage and poster tubes in the trunk. Time to hit the road.
entry.summary: Packing up and heading out to the Alberta Biomedical Engineering symposium in Kananaskis.

entry.title: The Reality of Muscle Fiber Isolation

link.title: The Reality of Muscle Fiber Isolation

entry.content:
Okay, the honeymoon phase of the thesis is officially over. I am finally starting to realize the actual scale of the work I signed up for.

I always knew muscle fibers were tiny since we see them in textbook diagrams all the time. But knowing that in theory and actually trying to isolate one under a microscope are very different things. Right now I am practicing with rabbit psoas tissue. Most of my day is just me hunched over a dissecting scope holding a pair of ridiculously sharp forceps.

I am quickly learning that my tweezer skills need a lot of work. When you are zoomed in that close, even just breathing too heavily makes your hands shake enough to look like an earthquake through the lens. You have to be incredibly careful to tease apart the tissue bundles fiber by fiber. If you slip up or pull too hard, you damage the myofilaments and ruin the sample.

The crazy part is watching my supervisor Venus do it. She can sit down at the scope and perfectly isolate and mount a fiber in about five minutes flat. It is honestly wild to watch and it really shows just how much experience she has. I will sit there for what feels like hours struggling while she just makes it look completely effortless.

It is definitely tedious and my eyes are feeling the strain. It takes a ton of patience, but when you finally manage to isolate a clean, intact fiber and get it mounted properly, it feels great. The actual mechanical testing is going to be a serious grind. At least my hands are getting steadier though.
entry.summary: A reality check as I begin the tedious process of isolating muscle fibers for my honors thesis.

entry.title: BME Symposium Dry Run
entry.content:
Today was the official dry run for my upcoming presentation at the Alberta Biomedical Engineering symposium. I am fully prepared to make my grand return to BME after a two year hiatus, so everyone better be ready.

I am presenting all the data I put together over the summer regarding the structural properties of female rat hearts following a high fat and high sucrose diet. To be completely honest, the poster itself is still looking a little shaky right now. Trying to condense months of tissue analysis and collagen concentration data into one cohesive, easy to follow story is proving to be harder than I thought.

The dry run with the lab definitely highlighted a few of those weak spots. I got a lot of feedback on my flow and how I am explaining the estrogen cardio-protection angle. It is always a little humbling to get critiqued, but I am taking it in stride. Their feedback was incredibly constructive and exactly what I needed to hear to actually make this presentation good.

There is definitely still progress being made despite the rough edges today. I have a very clear list of edits to tackle and enough time to iron out the remaining kinks before the actual symposium. It is a work in progress, but I know it is going to be solid by the time I am actually standing next to the board.
entry.summary: A reflection on my dry run presentation for the upcoming Alberta Biomedical Engineering symposium.

entry.title: Starting My Honors Thesis!
entry.content:
September is finally here, and I am absolutely buzzing with excitement to officially start my honors thesis! 🚀 After spending the summer presenting on the structural side of cardiac adaptations, I’m ready to take the next massive step: diving deep into the mechanical properties of the papillary muscle in male Sprague-Dawley rats.

Honestly, it feels a bit surreal. I’ve spent my whole Kinesiology degree looking forward to this moment, and getting to tackle this level of complex myofilament mechanics right here in the Herzog Lab is a dream come true. I know there are going to be some incredibly long days but I couldn’t be more ready for the challenge. Here’s to a massive final year—let’s get to work! 🔬🐀
entry.summary: Excited to officially begin my honors thesis diving deep into the mechanical properties of the papillary muscle.
